recurringPattern: Colored dot or pill badge (green=active/success, yellow=pending/warning, red=error/failed, gray=inactive/offline) with a short label. Often used in table columns, detail pages, and notification lists. Badges have rounded-full shape and uppercase or semibold text.
designHarm: The traffic-light color mapping is assumed universally but fails for accessibility (red-green colorblindness affects ~8% of users) and cultural context (red means 'good' in some Asian markets). The badge-over-text pattern prioritizes color recognition over clear communication — users must decode the hue, then read the label. When every status gets a badge, status transforms from signal to noise.
legitimateExceptions: System monitoring dashboards where second-resolution status recognition is critical
Deployment pipelines where the standard red/yellow/green mapping is an industry convention
possibleFixtures: Status system with icon+text instead of color dots
Status communicated through layout position and context instead of badges
originalObservations: AI-generated status systems always use the green-yellow-red-gray color mapping without considering accessibility needs.
Status badges always use pill/rounded-full styling even when other UI elements use squared corners.
The label text is always uppercase or semibold within the badge, reducing readability.
No alternative indicators (icons, patterns, text labels) accompany the color dots.
